eli3: Computers can't represent all real numbers exactly — they use floating-point with limited precision (usually 64 bits: 1 sign, 11 exponent, 52 mantissa). This leads to rounding errors in calculations.

Think of it like scientific notation: 6.022 × 10²³. The mantissa (6.022) has limited digits, so numbers get rounded. Small differences can be lost (catastrophic cancellation), and some operations accumulate error.
Understanding floating-point is crucial because seemingly simple computations (0.1 + 0.2 ≠ 0.3 in binary!) can fail, and algorithms that work in exact arithmetic may fail numerically.

whenWhy.title: Financial calculations
whenWhy.body: Decimal arithmetic critical for currency. Binary floating-point can't exactly represent 0.1. Use decimal types (Python's decimal, Java BigDecimal) or integer cents. Rounding modes and precision matter for compliance.

whenWhy.title: Machine learning optimization
whenWhy.body: Mixed precision training (FP16) speeds up deep learning but requires careful handling (loss scaling, master weights in FP32). Gradients can underflow in FP16. Numerical stability affects convergence.

whenWhy.title: Scientific computing
whenWhy.body: Accumulated rounding errors in long simulations (weather, molecular dynamics). Error analysis determines if results are trustworthy. Condition number and algorithm stability are as important as precision.

whenWhy.title: Comparing floating-point numbers
whenWhy.body: Never test equality (a == b). Use relative tolerance: |a-b| ≤ ε|b| or absolute tolerance for near-zero. Python's math.isclose(), numpy.allclose() implement proper comparison.

deepDiveIntro: The IEEE 754 standard defines how floating-point numbers are stored in bits. Double precision (FP64) uses 64 bits: one for the sign, 11 for the exponent (which determines scale), and 52 for the significant digits (mantissa). Machine epsilon is the smallest difference between 1.0 and the next representable number — about 2.2×10⁻¹⁶ for double precision — and bounds the relative rounding error of any single operation.

Single precision (FP32) has about 7 significant decimal digits, and half precision (FP16) has about 3, making it prone to underflow and overflow for gradients in deep learning. Special values include positive and negative infinity (produced by overflow or division by zero) and NaN (Not a Number, produced by invalid operations like zero divided by zero), which propagates through all subsequent calculations. Subnormal numbers fill the gap near zero at the cost of gradually reducing precision.

deepDive.heading: IEEE 754 Format
deepDive.body: A floating-point number is stored as three fields: a sign bit, an exponent that determines the scale (similar to the exponent in scientific notation), and a mantissa that stores the significant digits. Normal numbers always have an implicit leading 1 before the mantissa, giving an extra bit of precision for free. When the exponent field is all zeros, the number is subnormal — it loses the implicit leading 1 and precision degrades gracefully as the value approaches zero.

deepDive.heading: Machine Epsilon and ULP
deepDive.body: Machine epsilon is the gap between 1.0 and the next representable number — it tells you the worst-case relative rounding error of any single floating-point operation. For double precision this is about 2.22×10⁻¹⁶, meaning any result is accurate to about 16 significant decimal digits. The spacing between representable numbers grows proportionally to the number itself: near 1 billion, numbers are about 1 billion times further apart than near 1.

deepDive.heading: Catastrophic Cancellation
deepDive.body: When you subtract two numbers that are nearly equal, the leading significant digits cancel and you are left with only the low-order digits — which may be mostly rounding error. If both numbers are accurate to 16 digits but agree in the first 7, the difference has only 9 meaningful digits. This problem appears in variance computation (subtracting the square of the mean from the mean of squares) and in the quadratic formula when the discriminant is small.

deepDive.heading: Overflow and Underflow
deepDive.body: Overflow occurs when a result exceeds the largest representable value and becomes infinity. Underflow occurs when a result is smaller than the smallest normal number and either becomes zero or loses precision gradually. In machine learning, computing softmax of large logits can overflow the exponentials — the fix is to subtract the maximum logit before exponentiating, which does not change the result but keeps numbers in range. The log-sum-exp trick applies the same idea when computing the log of a sum of exponentials.

deepDive.heading: Decimal vs Binary Floating Point
deepDive.body: Binary floating-point cannot represent 0.1 or 0.2 exactly, because these are repeating fractions in binary — just as 1/3 repeats in decimal. This is why 0.1 + 0.2 does not equal exactly 0.3 in most programming languages. For financial calculations where exact decimal arithmetic is legally required, use decimal floating-point types (Python's decimal module or Java's BigDecimal) or store values as integers in the smallest unit (e.g., cents).

takeaways: IEEE 754: sign, exponent, mantissa. Double: 64 bits, ε ≈ 2.22×10⁻¹⁶.
Catastrophic cancellation: subtracting similar numbers loses precision.
Never compare floats with ==; use tolerance.
Overflow/underflow: use log-sum-exp trick, scaling for stability.

edgeCases.a: 0.1 in binary is infinite repeating (like 1/3 in decimal). With 52 bits, it's approximated. The small error accumulates in addition. Printing rounds to 12 digits hiding the issue; repr shows full precision: 0.30000000000000004. Use decimal or round() for display.

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between NaN and Infinity?
edgeCases.a: Infinity results from overflow (e.g., 1.0/0.0). Comparisons work: x < ∞ is True for finite x. NaN (Not a Number) results from invalid operations (0/0, ∞-∞, √-1). NaN ≠ NaN (only value not equal to itself). Propagates through calculations.

edgeCases.q: How does mixed precision training work?
edgeCases.a: Forward/backward in FP16 for speed, master weights in FP32 for precision. Loss scaling prevents gradient underflow (multiply loss, divide gradients). Some operations (BatchNorm, large reductions) stay in FP32. Tensor Cores on GPUs accelerate FP16 matrix multiply.

sources.label: Goldberg — What Every Computer Scientist Should Know About Floating-Point Arithmetic
